# Charter
In compliance with the newly approved City Ordinance No. 1, Series of 2025, which officially supersedes City Ordinance No. 7, Series of 2018, Gordon College affirms its full adherence to the updated directives as mandated by the local government. The institution remains committed to aligning its policies and operations with the latest regulatory framework to ensure a lawful, effective, and community-responsive higher educational institution.
[City-Ordinance-No.-01-s.-2025-An-Ordinance-Revising-the-Charter-of-Gordon-College](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/wp-content/uploads/2025/08/City-Ordinance-No.-01-s.-2025-An-Ordinance-Revising-the-Charter-of-Gordon-College.pdf)

# College Hymn
[![](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/play_button_gc-1.png)](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/gc-hymn.mp4)
The sea breeze over Subic Bay  
Moderate waves to mighty rest  
Sets our heart in inspiring pace  
How Gordon College made us best  
Raise our voices in silence  
Sing the praises in cadence  
Let these color our daily life  
And withstand the pilgrimage.  
Bless our Alma Mater  
Keep her firm and strong  
And be able to mold us all  
Set us upright on the road  
Hear the echo of Her song  
Feel the warmth of Her voice  
Keep the flame of the Torch  
At Gordon College proudly we belong.

# College Seal
This is the official college seal/logo of Gordon College. This is to be used in legal and formal documents and correspondence of the College. The colors green and yellow signify the community college’s commitment to sustainable development or a development for all generations as it works towards environmental sustainability in its attainment of its vision, mission and goals. “Ulo ng Apo” represents the city of Olongapo where the local Higher Education Institution is based. This symbolizes the ability of its students to lead and make a difference in the community.
The torch illuminates a book which represents the ability to serve as a guiding light for others. It also shows how education is a beacon of light that nurture learning. It is strategically placed beneath the Ulo ng Apo signifying that knowledge must be used, not for knowledge’s sake, but more for the community which includes its stakeholders.
The circular wreath made of interlocking branches and leaves represents the unity and strength of stakeholders and its students who stand among the distinguished scholars the local HEI molded and developed. With the wreath of laurel leaves being a symbol of honor and victory, students, faculty and the leaders of the institution are expected to achieve victory over ignorance while remaining honorable and preserving integrity towards excellence.
The platinum colored shield placed behind the Ulo ng Apo represents the boldness of its students in pursuit of new horizons of unparalleled intellectual success. To remind its students staff and all the members of the institution to be bold and brave enough to explore and seek new opportunities beyond the four corners of the college towards the development of the community they belong to.
Under the shield is the year that Gordon College was founded. This represents the number of years of upholding its values. Three values guide the college, namely: excellence; character and service which shall guide the institutions’ trifocal function of instruction, research and extension.
The logo is in a form of circle, symbolizing the perfect fulfillment of a holistic educational process.

# History of Gordon College
Gordon College was an off-shoot of the Olongapo City Training Center, then a training center for skilled manpower for employment at the former Naval facility of the United States. With the facility’s closure, and the slowing down of demand for skilled workers, City residents shifted to professional courses. The unavailability of affordable tertiary institutions for urban poor sector of the population led the City Government of Olongapo, during the incumbency of Hon. Katherine H. Gordon, then City Mayor, to open the Olongapo City Colleges through City Ordinance no. 9 series of 1999.
Initially, the College offered Bachelor of Science in Accountancy and Bachelor of Science in Computer Studies to 177 enrollees. Additional four-year programs were offered in the succeeding years to accommodate the demand of City residents for other programs.
City Ordinance no. 42 series of 2002 was passed in May 2002, providing a change in the name of Olongapo City Colleges to Gordon College, its present name, in honor of the Gordon family who greatly contributed to the development of the City of Olongapo. In the same year, the College was officially accepted as a member of the Association of Local Colleges and Universities (ALCU).
In 2004, a revision on the legal basis for the creation and operation of Gordon College was made through City Ordinance no. 36 series of 2004. In the same year, the College was granted by the Commission on Higher Education (CHED) Region III the certificate to operate with autonomy as per provisions of the Local Government Code of 19991. In addition to its undergraduate programs, the College opened its graduate programs in Education, Public Management and Business Administration.
In 2018, City Ordinance No. 07 series of 2018 was passed revising the charter of Gordon College subject to all Legal and Existing Rules and Regulations. This was superseded by City Ordinance No. 01 series of 2025 which was passed in 2025 that equipped Gordon College to expand its horizon for excellence to national and international level.

# Vision, Mission, Objectives, and Core Values
## Vision
A globally recognized local institution committed to innovative academic excellence, holistic and sustainable development, inclusivity, and community engagement.
## Mission
Produce empowered global citizens who create sustainable impact, uphold values of character, excellence, and service, and contribute to academic and societal development
## Strategic Objectives
1. **H**arness partnerships and linkages to produce graduates with global competencies aligned with the needs of the community and industry.
2. **O**rganize extension programs to sustain the greater impact of the institution to the community and environment.
3. **L**everage research outputs to improve existing knowledge and practices.
4. **I**ntegrate ethical principles and standards in the curriculum to strengthen the moral responsibilities of lifelong learners.
5. **S**upport continuous professional development and well-being of employees.
6. **T**ailor-fit student support services to ensure students thrive in the rapidly changing world.
7. **I**nvest in state-of-the-art facilities and technologies to enhance teaching, learning and research.
8. **C**ultivate a culture that embraces diversity and inclusivity.
## Core Values
* **Character.** Character is the foundation of a meaningful life. The institution strives to instill in students a strong moral compass, integrity, and a sense of responsibility for their actions. This includes fostering critical thinking skills, respect for diversity, and a commitment to a lifelong learning.
* **Excellence.** The institution provides a rigorous academic environment that challenges students to reach their full potential. Intellectual curiosity, innovation, and a pursuit of knowledge for its won sake is encouraged. It is believed that excellence is not just about achieving high grades, but also about developing the ability to analyze complex problems, think creatively, and communicate effectively.
* **Service.** Education empowers individuals to make a positive impact on their communities and the world. Students are encouraged to engage in service-learning opportunities that allow them to apply their knowledge and skills to real-world problems. The institution fosters a culture of social responsibility where students develop a sense of compassion and a desire to contribute to the betterment of society.

# Office of the College President
This office exercises overall responsibility and supervision over the College. The Chief Executive Officer of the College which is the College President is responsible for the articulation of the vision, mission, goals and objectives of the College and the development and implementation of educational programs and policies to achieve them. The office is directly accountable to the Board of Trustees for the organization, administration and supervision of the College.

# Continuing Students, Returnees and Cross Enrollment
## Continuing Students and Returnees
Continuing students are those who were previously enrolled in the College but unable to enroll for at least one semester and decided to continue studying in GC. These students have not requested for any transfer credential. They may have or have not filed for Leave of Absence.
Returning students are those who requested for transfer credentials but decided to re-enroll in the College. Returning students shall no longer be required to take the admission test.
## Cross Enrollment
* **GC Students to Enroll in Other Schools**
  + Students shall not be allowed to cross-enroll without proper consultation and consent from their respective College Deans. Students shall not be allowed to cross-enroll if the subject/course is not open during his/her last two semesters in the College. He/She shall request recommendation from his/her Dean and authorization coming from the College Registrar before he/she can cross-enroll to other colleges/universities.
* **Students from Other Schools to Cross-enroll in GC**
  + Students from other schools who would cross-enroll in GC should present a written permission from his school that he/she is allowed to enroll in GC.

# Freshmen Students
## Admission Requirements
Admission to Gordon College shall be open to Filipino citizens, both residents and non-residents of Olongapo City, Non-Filipino citizens, high school graduates, or passers of the high school equivalency certificate (PEPT), and shows evidence of being able to benefit from instruction. A NON-RESIDENT is defined as any individual who has lived less than one year in Olongapo City, is not registered to vote in Olongapo City and/or does not pay property taxes or contributes to the economic development of Olongapo City.
## New College Freshmen Students for Undergraduate Programs
* Admission to the chosen degree program will be based on an applicant’s Gordon College Admission Test (GCAT) results and previous high school/college academic records.
* Original copy of High School Report Card (F138/SF9) or Original ALS Certificate of Rating Secondary Level for ALS Passers
* Certificate of Good Moral Character
* Copy of Birth Certificate
* Medical Certificate (Issued by the Gordon College Clinic)
* Barangay Certificate of Residency
* One 2×2 picture

# IGS (Institute of Graduate Studies) Students
To qualify for admission to the Institute of Graduate Studies, an applicant must meet the following general and specific standards:
1. Student must have a minimum undergraduate GPA of 2.00 or 85% (except NSTP and PE). Student with lower general average than the prescribed rating may be admitted to the master’s program on probation. While on probationary status, s/he is allowed to carry the maximum load of only six (6) units per term. S/he will be placed in full standing status after having obtained an average rating of 1.5 or better in the first 12 units.
2. Specific programs may have different requirement as well as additional requirements such as materials and a personal interview, to wit:
   1. Master in Public Administration
      * At least two (2) years of government/ public service in any capacity
   2. Master in Business Management
      * BS Commerce or BS Business Administration graduate
      * Non-business course graduate must finish 18 tool courses. While earning these units, s/he may enroll any two basic/major subjects in the MBM program.
      * At least two (2) years of relevant experience/ service.
   3. Master of Arts in Education
      * Should be an education graduate
      * For non-education graduate, at least 24 units of professional education in the undergraduate level.
      * At least two (2) years of teaching experience/ service.
   4. Master of Arts in Nursing
      * Should be a Registered Nurse
      * At least two (2) years of experience in nursing and/or related field.
3. Pass the qualifying examination and interview.
4. All applicants for admission must submit the following;
   * Original Transcript of Records from recognized colleges or universities (with remarks, “Copy for Gordon College” or “Granted Honorable Dismissal”).
   * Two ID pictures (2×2/passport size)
   * Certificate of Employment
   * A Php200.00 non-refundable fee for application processing.
   * Additional materials, if required by specific program.

# Colleges
**Institute of Graduate Studies**
The mission of the Institute of Graduate Studies of Gordon College is to provide quality master’s degree programs and opportunities for continuing professional and career development for students whose academic preparation and personal characteristics foresee success in graduate studies. Each graduate program is firmly grounded in theoretical and empirical bodies of knowledge. Students are challenged to think critically and creatively, and to apply theories to practice in an atmosphere of inquiry and dynamic exchange with faculty and each other
**College of Allied Health Studies**
By 2025, the College of Allied Health Studies is envisioned to be a Center of Excellence in Allied Health Education in Region III and dedicated to being a forerunner in providing research-inclined instruction, evidence-based practice and community–responsive health care education committed to the holistic development of the human person.
**College of Business and Accountancy**
A center of excellence in accounting and business education that develops competitive leaders and professionals for providing professional training, innovative research and relevant extension services.
**College of Computer Studies**
The College of Computer Studies shall be the Center of Excellence in Information Technology and Computer Science for providing professional and technological training, scientific and innovative, and relevant community service
**College of Education, Arts and Sciences**
We envision the Gordon College-College of Education, Arts and Sciences to be a center of excellence in the field for providing professional training, innovative research and community service.
**College of Hospitality and Tourism Management**
To be a center of excellence in tourism and hospitality management with high standard through instruction, research and extension.

# Student Publication
**The Forefront** is the official student publication of Gordon College located in Olongapo City Sports Complex, Donor Street, East Tapinac, Olongapo City 2200. Its office is located at Rm. 516, 5th Floor, GC Main Building.
The Forefront was reestablished on June 2009, after a four-year hiatus, with the desire to promote the development and growth of responsible journalism as a means of strengthening ethical values, encouraging critical and creative thinking, and developing moral character and personal discipline in accordance with the Campus Journalism Act of 1991 (R.A. 7079).
**THE LOGO**
The official logo of the Forefront, as appeared above, is consist of two elements and two colors.
A *quill or pluma* refers to the writing profession. Its color, red, symbolizes courage and sacrifice. A *stem of laurel leaves* signifies triumph. Its color, golden yellow, symbolizes the nobility of the writing profession. The quill and the stem of laurel leaves form a capital letter “F” which stands for The Forefront.
**General Principles and Objectives**
The Forefront lives with its tagline, **“Informing and Empowering.”** To be able to uphold with its battlecry for campus press freedom, the Editorial Board sets the following General Principles:
1. The Forefront shall serve primarily as a newspaper of and for the students. It shall protect and promote the constitutionally mandated rights of students to free speech and of expression. In addition, it shall protect and promote the students’ rights and welfare.
2. The Forefront, being a publication of the students devoted to the ideals of truth, shall pursue the mission of social transformation through active and responsible journalism.
3. The Forefront, being an independent institution, shall not be subjected to any form of censorship. In turn, the editors and staff shall see to it that the Forefront exercises academic and campus press freedom in accordance with the laws of the Republic of the Philippines. It shall also seek to protect and promote  
   press freedom by taking firm and assertive stance against elements endangering such right.
4. The editors shall respect the rights of other parties that are equally protected by the laws of the Republic of the Philippines, affording them the right and opportunity to refute or reply to criticism or comment hurled at them.
Along with this principles, The Forefront shall at all times strive to:
1. Inform the public (i.e. the general population of the College, including the administration and the general public outside the College) with news of relevance and interest;
2. Serve as a venue for campus opinion and in maintaining an atmosphere of free and responsible discussion and of intellectual exploration;
3. Provide an outlet for students’ suggestions for the betterment of the campus;
4. Develop students’ powers of observation and discrimination concerning relative merits of news articles and;
5. Provide an opportunity for journalistic and creative writing.

# Online Application for GC Admission Test Procedure (1st Semester, A.Y. 2024-2025)
[Image: Online Application for GC Admission Test Procedure (1st Semester, A.Y. 2024-2025)]
[OSWS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/osws/)
**Monday, December 18, 2023**
**[Image: 🟢]
STEP 1. CREATE ONLINE PORTAL ACCOUNT**
1.1 Go to Gordon College Admission Portal login page (<https://gordoncollegeccs.edu.ph/gca>)
1.2 Click “Create Account”
1.3 Type your email address, given name, last name, date of birth, contact number, desired password and select type of student (New Student, Transferee, Second Courser)
1.4 Click “Register”
Notes:
o A valid and existing email address is required in this process.
o Do not create multiple accounts as this will be a ground for disapproval of application.
· Do not forget your email and password
**[Image: 🟢]
STEP 2. LOG ON TO YOUR ACCOUNT**
2.1 Go to the portal login page
2.2 Type your previously registered email address and password then click “Log In”
2.3 You will be directed to the portal Dashboard.
2.4 The Dashboard displays the status of your application.
[Image: 🟢]
 **STEP 3. PROFILING**
3.1 On the upper left side of the page, click “Upload Photo” to upload your recent photo.
Guide:
o 2×2 colored picture, white background
o Formal pose wearing collared top, No eyeglasses or any accessories like earrings.
o Picture should be taken not later than one week prior to filing of application.
3.2 On the Menu, click “Profile”
3.3 Completely and accurately fill out the forms (Personal Information, Family Background and Desired Programs)
3.4 Read the Gordon College General Data Privacy Notice and click the CHECKBOX.
3.5 Review the form and click “Submit”
**[Image: 🟢]
STEP 4. UPLOADING OF DOCUMENTS**
4.1 On the Menu, click “Uploads”
4.2 Upload scanned or clear image of the required documents. Click the placeholder to browse for an image to upload.
4.3 The image file should be less than 2MB to avoid uploading errors
Required documents:
o Recent Good Moral Character Certificate
o PSA Authenticated Birth Certificate
o Senior High School ID or Any Valid ID Card
– Click “Submit” to finalize the uploading.
[Image: 🟢]
 **STEP 5. GCAT Schedule**
5.1 The schedule of GCAT will be posted to your portal account within three (3) working days after submission of COMPLETE application and required documents.
5.2 Upon posting of the test schedule on the online portal, you may download and print the following:
o GCAT Schedule slip
o Student Information Sheet
————————————————–
What to bring on the test day?
1. GCAT Schedule Slip
2. Student Information Sheets
3. Senior High School or any valid ID Card
5. Pencil and Pen
Note: Please be informed that all applications shall be submitted online to prevent the spread of Covid-19.

These newly crowned icons are now set to carry the GCian pride to the regional and national stage, representing the institution in the Association of Local Colleges and Universities (ALCU) and the Local Colleges and Universities Athletic Association (LCUAA). | via Kana Maye Dueñas, The FOREFRONT

# Gordon College Receives 21 Certificates of Accreditation at NNQAA 2026 Summit
[Image: Gordon College Receives 21 Certificates of Accreditation at NNQAA 2026 Summit]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Friday, March 6, 2026**
Gordon College proudly received Certificates of Accreditation for its 21 ALCUCOA-accredited programs during the NNQAA 2nd Quality Assurance Summit and Conferment Ceremony for Accredited Programs and Institutions 2026, with the theme “Responsible Future: Quality Assurance in Action.”
The recognition was conferred by the National Network of Quality Assurance Agencies (NNQAA), Inc., led by Chairman Dr. Raymundo P. Arcega, during the summit held March 6, 2026 at The Heritage Hotel Manila.
The award was received by Dr. Imelda D.P. Soriano, College President; Dr. Kristin Joy A. Mendoza, Vice President for Institutional Planning, Development and External Affairs; and Mr. Joseph Angelo B. Pusing, Officer-in-Charge of the Center for Quality Assurance and Accreditation, representing Gordon College.
These recognitions highlight the College’s continued commitment to quality education, academic excellence, and institutional development.
Level III Accredited Programs
1. Bachelor of Science in Business Administration – Financial Management
2. Bachelor of Science in Business Administration – Human Resource Management
3. Bachelor of Science in Business Administration – Marketing Management
4. Bachelor of Science in Customs Administration
5. Bachelor of Secondary Education – Mathematics
6. Bachelor of Secondary Education – Social Studies
7. Bachelor of Science in Nursing
Level II Accredited Programs
1. Bachelor of Elementary Education
2. Bachelor of Science in Accountancy
3. Bachelor of Science in Computer Science
4. Bachelor of Secondary Education – English
5. Bachelor of Secondary Education – Science
6. Bachelor of Science in Information Technology
Level I Accredited Programs
1. Bachelor of Arts in Communication
2. Bachelor of Early Childhood Education
3. Bachelor of Secondary Education – Filipino
4. Bachelor of Science in Hospitality Management
5. Bachelor of Science in Midwifery
6. Bachelor of Science in Tourism Management
Candidate Status Programs
1. Bachelor of Culture and Arts Education
2. Bachelor of Physical Education
The College extends its deepest gratitude to Hon. Rolen C. Paulino Jr., City Mayor; Hon. Cristabelle Marie C. Paulino, Chairperson of the Committee on Education; Dr. Imelda D.P. Soriano, College President; the City Officials, the Board of Trustees, Vice Presidents, College Officials, Deans, Assistant Deans, Program Chairs, Management Council Members, Faculty Members, Administrative Personnel, and all our stakeholders for their continued support in strengthening Gordon College’s pursuit of quality assurance and academic excellence.

# PENCIL PASSED: TF Ushers in New Leaders, Journos
[Image: PENCIL PASSED: TF Ushers in New Leaders, Journos]
[ForeFront](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/forefront/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Saturday, September 13, 2025**
In a time-honored tradition of the passing of the pencil, the outgoing editorial board of The Forefront for academic year 2024-2025 has turned over their duties to the new set of editorial board for the incoming academic year in a turnover ceremony and culminating activity held last Thursday, September 11, 2025, at the Physical Education Hall, Gordon College – Main Campus.
After 53 days of deliberation, fourth-year Bachelor of Arts in Communication (BACOMM) student and outgoing managing editor (Man-Ed) Mx. John Patrick Mateo was hailed as the new Editor-in-Chief (EIC) and OIC news editor of The Forefront for Academic Year 2025-2026, replacing outgoing editor-in-chief and BACOMM alumna, Ms. Bealyn Demanalata.
“Out of all the speeches I have delivered, this one took three years in the making—three years of service to the community, to the people, and most especially, to the marginalized and oppressed,” Ms. Demanalata said in her step-down speech.
The outgoing EIC further emphasized the role of campus journalists as torchbearers of the truth. “As you carry this legacy forward, may you remember that every article written, every truth defended, and every voice amplified is part of a greater fight—a fight not just for recognition, but for democracy, justice, and the people. This is the mark we leave behind: that we dared, that we endured, and that we surpassed the limits.”
Formally assuming their new role, Mx. Mateo stressed that, despite their imperfection, they will remain to be an empathetic and transformational leader for the publication. “Bilang huling pagkakataon na maging student journalist, wala akong planong perpekto. Ngunit, pangako, hindi ako magiging makasarili’t hahatiin ko ang aking puso para lamang maiabot ang pagmamahal sa bawat isa sa inyo,” the new EIC said.
Together with publication alumni Mr. John Carlo Bermudez (outgoing Associate Editor), Mr. I-man Anthonie Klay Garcia (outgoing Circulations Manager), Mr. Emar Kristian Aranda (outgoing OIC Opinion-Editorial Editor), Ms. Patrisha Marie Pineda (outgoing DevComm Editor), and Ms. Jessica Dimatulac (outgoing Head Layout Artist), their duties and responsibilities were officially turned over to the new editorial board, who each gave their respective turnover speeches.
Moreover, forming Mx. Mateo’s executive committee are: Mx. Lance Isaac Leon, outgoing head of visual arts, as associate editor; Ms. Mikhaela Sagun as managing editor who remains as editor of the literary and culture section, and; Mr. Rishen David Belza as circulations manager and head broadcaster.
Meanwhile, the editorial board was appointed based on the results of the editorial board exam last July 7, 2025 as well as performance during the previous academic year. Seated on the board are:
– News Editor: Mx. John Patrick Mateo
– Opinion and Editorial Editor: Ms. Stephanie Estrella
– Feature Editor: Ms. Shermel Carlos
– Development Communication Editor: Ms. Ma. Althea Zyrene Ramos
– Literary and Cultura Editor: Ms. Mikhaela Sagun
– Sports Editor: Ms. Alyssa Marie Alcantara
– Head Broadcaster: Mr. Rishen David Belza
– Senior Broadcaster: Ms. Jane Weslie Dela Cruz
– Head Photojournalist: Ms. Lauren Louise Sanita
– Head of Traditional Arts: Ms. Mary Isobelle Arcilla
– Head of Digital Arts: Ms. Rich Mae Tianchon
– Head Graphic Artist: Mr. Zeyrus Enriquez
– Head Layout Artist: Ms. Samantha Sunga
– Head Multimedia Artist: Mr. Ian Joshua Umali
– Social Media Manager: Mr. John Christian Rocero
Following the ceremony, the recruits who had passed the competitive recruitment exam formally took their journalist oath, led by the newly minted EIC. Also addressing the current and outgoing editorial board, together with the continuing staffers, are Mr. Guiller Marila (Student Publication Coordinator) with a special message from the college president, Dr. Imelda DP Soriano, delivered by Mr. Joseph Pusing (Quality Assurance Coordinator). | via Lance Isaac Leon/The FOREFRONT

# Bagong Lakan at Lakambini ng Kasaysayan, pinasinayaan sa Gordon College
[Image: Bagong Lakan at Lakambini ng Kasaysayan, pinasinayaan sa Gordon College]
[CEAS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/ceas/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Sunday, September 7, 2025**
GORDON COLLEGE—Inukit na ang mga bagong lakan at lakambini ng kasaysayan kasabay ng panapos na selebrasyon ng Buwan ng Kasaysayan 2025 na may temang “Diwa ng Kasaysayan, Kabilin sa Kabataan” na idinaos noong Agosto 30, sa Gordon College Physical Education (PE) Hall sa pangunguna ng Samahan ng mga Mag-aaral sa Araling Panlipunan (SMAP).
Nagwagi sina G. Lorenzen Rob Gonzales mula sa BSEd Soc 3-A at Bb. Angel Mae Deseo mula sa BSEd Soc 1-A para sa Lakan at Lakambini ng Kasaysayan 2025.
Nasungkit naman nina G. Jhan Girbaud Hipolito mula sa BSEd Soc 2-A at Bb. Jhade Aizelle Quiocho mula sa BSEd Soc 1-A ang korona bilang Ginoong Unang Gantimpala at Binibining Unang Gantimpala.
Namayagpag din bilang Ginoong Ikalawang Gantimpala at Binibining Ikalawang Gantimpala sina G. Rafael Ellado mula sa BSEd Soc 1-A at Bb. Gervy Sarmiento mula sa BSEd Soc 2-A.
Bukod sa Lakan at Lakambini 2025, iginawad din ang mga espesyal na parangal tulad ng Lunting Diwa at Best in Talent na parehong nakuha nina G. Jhan Girbaud Hipolito at Bb. Jhade Aizelle Quiocho.
Nakamit naman nina G. Lorenzen Rob Gonzales at Bb. Jhade Aizelle Quiocho ang mga titulong Best in Formal Attire at People’s Choice Award.
Samantala, inuwi naman nina Bb. Angel Mae Deseo at G. Lorenzen Rob Gonzales ang karangalang Binibini at Ginoo ng Nakaraan.
“Nung sinabi na ako yung panalo, naging proud ako kasi nagbunga yung suporta ng pamilya at mga kaibigan, higit sa lahat, nagbunga yung effort at tiwala ko sa sarili” saad ng Lakan 2025.
“In-expect ko talaga na may chance akong manalo.” Ibinahagi din ni Lorenzen na pinaghirapan niya ang kanyang preparasyon, mula ensayo hanggang pagpapakilala at pagsagot sa tanong ng mga hurado.
Samantala, labis naman ang pasasalamat ni Bb. Angel Mae Deseo, Lakambini 2025, sa lahat ng tumulong sa kanya lalo na at noong panahong iyon ay masama pa ang kanyang karamdaman.
“Tinrangkaso po ako hanggang sa mismong laban, sa kabila nun ay ipinagpatuloy ko ang laban sapagkat nakikita ko ang aking mga kamag-aral (SOC 1-A) na nagtutulong-tulong upang matapos ang lahat ng aming kakailanganin,” saad ni Angel Mae Deseo.
“Lahat ng pagod at puyat ay naging makabuluhan sapagkat nasungkit namin ang korona at karangalan,” aniya. | via Lovely Doroin/The FOREFRONT

# From Debits to Dominance: Pink Team (cPINKa) Credits the Ultimate Gamble Victory
[Image: From Debits to Dominance: Pink Team (cPINKa) Credits the Ultimate Gamble Victory]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Sunday, March 1, 2026**
Accountancy students of Gordon College gathered at East Tapinac Covered Court on February 25 for Junior Philippine Institute of Accountants (JPIA)’s Ultimate Gamble Team Building.
The participants collaborated, competed, and conquered through strategy-driven games that tested teamwork and unity.
Demy Anne M. Roallos headed the Green Team (Berde Makausad), Triskelly Lianne Cyril Cabildo led the Red Team (Pulang Ina), Felicity Dyle Rivas guided the Yellow Team (Bibeni Luca), Aran Benz C. Sardañas captained the Orange Team (Ilocos Empanada), Shakira Nicole B. Guintu steered the Pink Team (cPINKa – Certified Pink Accountants), Jan Lester M. Bitangcol led the Purple Team (Ooh-Lila), and Hannah Jhem M. Malit directed the Blue Team (BIR – Bluereau of Impeccable Recorders).
As the event began, each team performed their energetic yells and presented their flags to start the event with a blast. In the first game entitled “Caterpillar Game,” Green Team (Berde Makausad) won first place, Orange Team (Ilocos Empanada) gained second place, and Yellow Team (Bibeni Luca) took third place.
For the second game of “Pass the Balloon,” Red Team (Pulang Ina) achieved the first place, followed by the Pink Team (cPINKa), and lastly, the Yellow Team (Bibeni Luca). As the third game commenced, entitled “The Floor is Lava,” Pink Team (cPINKa) was hailed as the champion, Green Team (Berde Makausad) as the first runner-up, and the Orange Team (Ilocos Empanada) as the second runner-up.
Moreover, in the station games, the teams played in seven stations, namely Pass the Ball, Cut it Up, Wrap-tastic, Balloon Cup Relay, Rollercoaster Ball, T-Shirt Relay, and Calcu Relay. Yellow Team (Bibeni Luca) triumphed in first place, Purple Team (Ooh-Lila) succeeded in second place, while Pink Team (cPINKa) acquired third place.
After consolidating all performance entries like preparing a final financial statement, Pink Team (cPINKa) emerged as the overall champion with a total audited score of 185 points, followed by Green Team (Berde Makausad) with 180 points, Yellow Team (Bibeni Luca) with 175 points, Blue Team (BIR) and Red Team (Pulang Ina) who tied with 170 points, and lastly, Orange Team (Ilocos Empanada) and Purple Team (Ooh-Lila) who both garnered 150 points. Furthermore, Blue Team (BIR) won for the best yell, while Red Team (Pulang Ina) claimed the best flag award.
According to Pink (cPINKa) team leader, Shakira Nicole B. Guintu, their victory came from proper planning before the activities and going all-in on collaboration. “No’ng nag-activity po kami, laging may nagsasalita and nakikinig. Pwedeng higher batch, pwedeng lower batch, basta lahat naman po kami nakikipag-cooperate,” said Guintu.
Guintu also added that the team building reflected real-world challenges in accountancy, helping them to be patient and to remain calm under pressure. “May activities na parang paulit-ulit na kami pero nire-remind namin na kalmahan lang, na i-go lang sa flow namin, sa pace namin,” the team leader stated.
The activity ultimately reinforced that future accountants must not only master numbers but also embody the 3 C’s—Collaborate, Compete, and Conquer—skills essential in navigating real-world professional engagements and organizational challenges. | via Justine Stephanie A. Canlas/The FOREFRONT

# 3rd Distinguished Alumni Service Award: Call for Nomination 2024
[Image: 3rd Distinguished Alumni Service Award: Call for Nomination 2024]
[Alumni](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/alumni/)
**Wednesday, January 24, 2024**
## THE 3RD GORDON COLLEGE DISTINGUISHED ALUMNI SERVICE AWARD (GC-DASA) FOR THE YEAR 2024
**GENERAL INFORMATION AND GUIDELINES**
Pursuant to Gordon College Alumni Association, Inc. (GCAAI) Board Resolution No. 4, series of 2022, the GCAAI establishes the **DISTINGUISHED ALUMNI SERVICE AWARD (DASA)** to honor Gordon College graduates who have excelled both in their professional and personal careers and who have served as an example to other alumni, students, and the community, both locally and globally. Starting this year, the GCAAI Board of Trustees will recognize the exemplary service and distinguished achievements that brought honor to the Institution and have made significant contributions to society. The GC-DASA recipients will exemplify the core values that the Institution uphold since its establishment: **Exceptional Character, Remarkable Excellence, And Unselfish Service.**
The GC-DASA shall be the highest honor bestowed by the Alumni Association and will be an event to be celebrated annually as part of the GC Founding Anniversary and/or GC Alumni Homecoming.
## GENERAL GUIDELINES:
1. Only graduates of Gordon College (or GC, formerly Olongapo City Colleges) are eligible for the GC-DASA. The nominee shall be a member of the Gordon College Alumni Association, Inc. as confirmed by its Board.
2. Any GC graduate, faculty member, or stakeholders may make nominations for the GC-DASA. Self–nominations are accepted. The nominee shall accomplish the Official Nomination Form to be provided by the GCAAI. No limitation will be placed on the total number of nominations accepted for consideration.
3. The nominee shall have no pending administrative or criminal case upon nomination. He/she shall exhibit a high degree of character & integrity.
4. Only one recipient per college/program will be awarded. In case the college/program will have no nominees/recipients, the awarding ceremony will still commence. Likewise, the GCAAI Board of Trustees can directly, without prejudice to the respective college, nominate and evaluate for such an award though the college/program have already a candidate.
5. Only living candidates are considered. In the event of the death of a candidate between notification of the award and the award ceremony, the award may be given posthumously with the permission of the family and the GCAAI. In the event that the awardee declines to accept the award and/or fails to attend the awarding ceremonies, the GCAAI reserves the right to withdraw the award.
6. A GC graduate sitting as a member of the selection committee or a member of the GCAAI Board of Trustees is not eligible to receive the award. This individual is eligible for nomination after a one-year waiting period from the date he/she step down from his/her role.
7. Recipients are only eligible for one GC-DASA in a lifetime.
8. The recipients will receive a plaque or certificate of recognition and will be posted on the official FB page of the GCAAI. It will announce the annual recipients through various methods and honor each recipient with the appropriate recognition.
9. If for any reason, the GCAAI is not able to conduct the Awards as planned (including due to tampering, unauthorized intervention, fraud, technical difficulties, pandemic, or any other causes beyond the control of the organizer which impact the administration, security, fairness, integrity or proper conduct of the Awards), then they reserve the right in their sole discretion to make any reasonable variation to the Awarding Ceremony.
10. The Nomination Committee and/or GCAAI Board of Trustees may disqualify any nominee that they reasonably determine has breached any of the terms and conditions or otherwise engaged in unlawful or other improper conduct that may bring the Awards into disrepute or jeopardize the fair and proper conduct of the Awards.
11. Other guidelines may be promulgated as decided by the GCAAI Board of Trustees.
## NOMINATION COMMITTEE:
The Nomination Committee (NC) may be organized by the Dean/OIC in every college and/or the GCAAI. The committee must be composed of at least three (3) members from their respective colleges. The total count of committee members should be an odd number. The chairperson of the NC shall be the Dean or its duly authorized representative and for the GCAAI, any member can initiate such nomination. No member of the NC must be related within the second degree of consanguinity or affinity to any of the nominees/candidates for awards. If the Dean/OIC is related within the second degree of consanguinity or affinity to any of the candidates for awards, the Dean/OIC must inhibit him/herself from participating in the process. The approval shall come from the person next in rank.
The NC shall:
1. Abide by the criteria set forth by the GCAAI.
2. For nominees endorsed by the College, it shall issue a certificate of authenticity to all the documents that will be submitted to the GCAAI.
3. Deliberate on the qualifications of the nominees for the awards based on the criteria and the documents submitted.
4. Recommend to the GCAAI Board of Trustees or its Alumni Association President the result of nomination for approval.
5. Recommend to the GCAAI Board of Trustees or its Alumni Association President the resolution on any related issue that may arise from the results of the awards.
6. Ensure that the guidelines stipulated herein are followed.
## NOMINATION AND SELECTION PROCESS:
1. Complete the online/hard copy Nomination Form. This also forms part of the terms and conditions.
2. Nominators will receive an auto-generated copy via email of what they submitted, i.e., the nomination form including any attachments. In any case, it is not available, the NC may determine what means of notification it will conduct to the nominators to confirm the submission of the nomination form and its attachments.
3. Nominees and nominators will be contacted by the NC/GCAAI within two days of receipt of the nomination to acknowledge receipt.
4. A nominee may be asked to clarify or submit further information and evidence to assist the NC/GCAAI in assessing the nomination at any stage of the selection process.
5. The NC/GCAAI may also seek verification of the nominee’s achievements from their current or previous employer or other relevant individual or organization representative. By being nominated for this Award, the nominees are giving consent to the NC/GCAAI to contact these individuals to verify nominations.
6. Nominations will be reviewed and shortlisted by the NC/GCAAI and nominees who have not been shortlisted will be notified. The GC-DASA candidates will be asked to submit a brief profile for this purpose. The candidate shall garner a total score of 60 points to be included in the shortlist. If a nominee cannot meet the required points, he/she is automatically excluded from the shortlist.
7. Every member of the NC will recommend/endorse the final candidate to the GCAAI President and then, the GCAAI Board of Trustees, sitting as Awards Selection Committee, will issue a confirmation through a Board Resolution. The report on the results of the NC shall be signed by all members of the committee and certified by the chairperson.
8. In any case that such protest will be filed during the confirmation of the final candidates, the final decision on the protest shall be decided by the GCAAI Board of Trustees. The GCAAI Board of Trustees’ decision is final on all matters relating to the nomination process and the selection of award recipients, and they are not required to provide feedback as to why a nomination was unsuccessful.

# LIMC Services
## **READER’S / CIRCULATION SERVICES**
Readers/circulation services are provided to keep the library patrons in the use of information resources and facilities. It includes services such as lending and retrieving of books, assisting in literature search, locating books, shelving, recalling books that were checked out.
**Function**
1. To serve promptly and efficiently all library patrons.
2. To substitute when possible, suitable and adequate materials if the desired materials is not available or charged out.
3. To assist students and other library patrons in using the card catalog – OPAC and teach them the importance as the index to the collection of books in the library.
4. To become acquainted with the interests, problems, capacities and needs of the students and faculty.
5. To provide efficient service.
## **CIRCULATION PROCESS**
Circulation is the primary task of the libraries. Its objective is to maximize the utilization of all materials in order to serve a great number of library users. To achieve this, an effective charging system is established.
**Activities**
1. Require all students who are officially enrolled to present their school ID.
2. Check in and check out books and other information materials.
3. Assist readers in the use of the library catalog –OPAC and print/card, and explain the arrangement of books on the shelves.
4. File and maintain patron’s record.
5. Keep statistical records to provide a basis for understanding, planning and carrying out the circulation job in terms of users’ demand.
6. Assist/help students/users in the selection of books and other materials for reading.
7. Control the material collection so that the availability of materials to the patron is maximized.
8. Shelf-read books in stacks to ensure proper placement and order in shelving.
## **CHARGING/ DISCHARGING SERVICES**
A library, no matter how it is, must adopt its own system in charging and discharging library materials in order to record and trace the borrowers of the book
**Procedure**
1. In borrowing a book, one presents his or her school ID to the librarian and asks for the book/s he or she wishes to borrow.
2. The borrower signs the library card and book card attached to the book.
3. The library card and the book card are clipped together and filed in the charging box alphabetically.
4. The staff in charge will log details of the borrowed book/s in the log book.
5. When a book is returned, the book card and the library card are pulled out from the file of loaned-out books. The borrower card will give back to the student with a remark of returned and the date when the book is returned.
6. Put a remarks on a book card when the book is return.
7. Return the book card into the book pocket and return it to the shelves.
## **REFERENCE BOOK SERVICE**
Reference service handles inquiry and assists user in find the information they require, access it, and use it to meet their needs. According to Kumar (2003), reference service helps to establish contact between a user and the right document at the right time, thereby saving the time of the user.
**A. Objective of the Reference:**  
The main objective of reference service is to provide the library patrons access to the information resources. The reference work is directly concerned with assistance to users’ information and in using the resources in their study and research. To achieve this objective, the librarian provides the information promptly and efficiently necessary to satisfy the patron’s request.
**Activities**
1. Conduct reference interviews to understand and determine the needs of the clientele for quick sighting of information.
2. Give information and answer factual questions.
3. Help the patron find the needed information and provide guidance in the choice of materials.
4. Give formal and informal instruction on the tools of the library catalog – OPAC, indexes and others.
5. Answer reference questions requiring normal and extended searching.
6. Retrieve reference questions handled for possible future solutions.
7. Guide students in their interest with books and teach them how to love books and to make books an important part of their life;
8. Retrieve reference statistics.
9. Maintain information files.
**B****. Use of OPAC**
1. The student types the subject he/she wants to research.
2. Click SEARCH and jot down the information about the book.
3. Don’t click other keys so as not to destroy the system.
## **LIBRARY ORIENTATION AND INSTRUCTION**
Library orientation refers to helping students feel aware of and comfortable with the facilities and the services available, with the location of collections, and with the people who service those collections. At every start of the school year, the library staff, in coordination with the office of the student welfare and services, give orientation to the freshmen and transferees during the general orientation program.
**Objectives:**
1. To stimulate interest in the use of library information resource.
2. To maximize the use of references and other materials for their research work and study.
3. To help them instill the habit of self-education through books and other materials.
**Activities**
1. Give class orientation to all new students. This orientation includes the following:  
   – introduction of library personnel  
   – library sections  
   – library services  
   – arrangement of books and other materials in the library  
   – rules and regulations  
   – classification system used  
   – library catalog  
   – OPAC: its importance and use
2. Bring the students to the library for familiarization of its various sections and resources.
## **PHOTOCOPYING SERVICES**
Policies in Photocopying of Materials:
1. Photocopying of complete books and theses or dissertations is not allowed. Only the abstract of the theses or dissertations may be photocopied.
2. Users are responsible for complying with the copyright act/and intellectual property rights.
3. For materials to be photocopied outside the library, the client should fill out the library card and submit them together with their school ID to the librarian in-charge. (This is only allowed when the photocopy service is not available inside the library).
## **INTERLIBRARY LOAN/ REFERRAL SERVICES**
Procedure:
1. The student is advised to exhaust first our resources before going out to other libraries.
2. If the references or the materials are not available, the student is advised to fill out a request form for a referral letter.
3. The librarian will issue a referral letter and give instruction or advise the student what to do in other libraries before she signs.
## **INTERNET SERVICES**
This section is equipped with computers to gain access to information on the Internet. Faculty and students can use this section to search for materials available in the library using subscribed and free online databases.
Procedure:
1. Approach the library staff for the availability of computer units.
2. The library follows the “first come, first served” policy.
3. Sign into the Computer Usage Form
4. Proceed the computer unit, the library staff will set the time for you.
5. The library limits the time a client can use the computer to one (1) hour (but can exceed if there are no other users waiting) to maximize its utilization and serve many students.
## **BULLETIN BOARD DISPLAY**
Activities:
1. The librarian makes a complete list of all newly acquired materials.
2. The theme of the display for the bulletin board is determined.
3. The librarian plans the design of the display.
4. The librarian gathers all the needed materials for the display.
5. The librarian prepares and displays the material in the bulletin board.
Priorities and Guidelines in the Display:
* Priority is given to the library who owns the bulletin board.
* Notices and publications will be under the prerogative of the Head Librarian using the best interest to the Gordon College community as his reference point.  
  The following types of information will not be allowed for display:
  + Posters promoting political parties advocating the election of any candidate or a stand in any issue concerning elections.
  + Notices of merchandise or services for sale
  + Rental announcements
  + The appearance and content of the notice must be acceptable or suitable for display.
  + Generally, notices will not be posted for longer than three (3) weeks

# BLEPT 2025 Yields 200 New GC-Produced Professional Teachers
[Image: BLEPT 2025 Yields 200 New GC-Produced Professional Teachers]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, December 22, 2025**
The College of Education, Arts, and Sciences (CEAS) beams with pride as it yields 39 Licensed Professional Teachers (LPTs) for the Elementary level and 161 new LPTs for the Secondary level at this year’s Board Licensure Examination for Professional Teachers (BLEPT)
Gordon College (GC) once again surpassed the national passing rates in the Board Licensure Examination for Professional Teachers (BLEPT) conducted on September 21 and November 30, 2025.
For the Elementary Level, 39 out of 49 Gordon College examinees passed the LEPT, resulting in a passing rate of 79.59% against a national passing rate of 51.04%.
Meanwhile, for the Secondary Level, 161 out of 177 Gordon College examinees passed the LEPT, posting an overall passing rate of 90.96%, with 50% of second-takers also passing.
Overall, 76,696 out of 122,528 examinees nationwide passed the September–November 2025 BLEPT, representing a national passing rate of 65.04% for the secondary level.
Notably, the Board Licensure Examination for Professional Teachers (BLEPT) was rescheduled in Regions I, II and Cordillera Administrative Region (CAR) due to inclement weather.
The examinations for the said regions were conducted on November 30. | via Lovely Doroin/The FOREFRONT

# Gordon College Faculty Join Basic FSL Training to Promote Inclusive Education
[Image: Gordon College Faculty Join Basic FSL Training to Promote Inclusive Education]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Friday, August 29, 2025**
Faculty members from the College of Education, Arts and Sciences (CEAS) and the College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) with Mr. Joseph Atencio-OIC, Head of Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (DEI) Unit proudly took part in a three-day Basic Filipino Sign Language (FSL) Training held last August 26–28, 2025 at the SMX Convention Center, Olongapo City. The event was organized by the Olongapo City Persons with Disability Affairs Office (PDAO) in collaboration with the Department of Education – Olongapo City Division Office.
Throughout the training, participants engaged in interactive and hands-on activities designed to equip educators with the skills to communicate effectively with the Deaf—nurturing a more inclusive, and compassionate learning environment.
This initiative not only strengthened our instructors’ professional competencies but also reaffirmed their dedication to accessibility, diversity, and inclusivity in education—a true reflection of Gordon College’s commitment to ensuring that no learner is left behind.

# 3 studes earn top spots in CFMS and CMMS exams; GC produces new CPACE-certified undergrads
[Image: 3 studes earn top spots in CFMS and CMMS exams; GC produces new CPACE-certified undergrads]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Tuesday, December 23, 2025**
Francis Jerome De Jesus and Belynda Apog, 4th-year Bachelor of Science in Business Administration Major in Financial Management students, ranked 4th and 5th, respectively, in their assessment examinations. Meanwhile, Marketing Management major Michaella Soliven secured the 10th spot of her own.
In a post, the Center for Professional Advancement and Continuing Education, Inc. (CPACE Philippines) announced the passers of the Batch 25 Certified Financial Management Specialist (CFMS)® and Certified Marketing Management Specialist (CMMS)® assessment examinations on Thursday, December 18, and Friday, December 19, respectively.
De Jesus, 90.42%, and Apog, 90%, both broke into the Top 10 for the CFMS® assessment exam. On the flip side, Soliven cleared the CMMS® assessment with a rating of 86.67%.
Furthermore, 78 additional Financial Management from the College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) students cleared the assessment, bringing the institutional passing rate to 76.70%, while the results also saw one more taker pass the CMMS® exam.
CPACE Philippines totaled an overall passing rate of 69.88% and 80% for CFMS® and CMMS® examinations, respectively.
The assessment examinations were conducted via online and in-person formats, including Gordon College (GC) – Annex Bldg., from November 28 through December 13. | via Keane Rafael Abdon/The FOREFRONT

# 𝐏𝐚𝐠𝐥𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐩𝐚𝐭 𝐧𝐠 𝐤𝐚𝐩𝐚𝐧𝐠𝐲𝐚𝐫𝐢𝐡𝐚𝐧 𝐬𝐚 𝐛𝐚𝐠𝐨𝐧𝐠 𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐦𝐢𝐧𝐨, 𝐩𝐨𝐫𝐦𝐚𝐥 𝐧𝐚𝐧𝐠 𝐧𝐚𝐢𝐬𝐚𝐤𝐚𝐭𝐮𝐩𝐚𝐫𝐚𝐧
[Image: 𝐏𝐚𝐠𝐥𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐩𝐚𝐭 𝐧𝐠 𝐤𝐚𝐩𝐚𝐧𝐠𝐲𝐚𝐫𝐢𝐡𝐚𝐧 𝐬𝐚 𝐛𝐚𝐠𝐨𝐧𝐠 𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐦𝐢𝐧𝐨, 𝐩𝐨𝐫𝐦𝐚𝐥 𝐧𝐚𝐧𝐠 𝐧𝐚𝐢𝐬𝐚𝐤𝐚𝐭𝐮𝐩𝐚𝐫𝐚𝐧]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Friday, July 18, 2025**
GORDON COLLEGE — Isinalin na ang mga posisyon at kapangyarihan sa mga bagong opisyales, gayundin ang pagpapakilala sa mga bagong kakatawan sa mga GCians sa katatapos na Supreme Student Council Turnover Ceremony na ginanap sa PE Hall nitong ika-14 ng Hulyo.
Nasaksihan din ng mga lider-estudyante mula sa iba’t ibang organisasyon sa loob ng institusyon ang nabanggit na seremonya.
Bilang panimula, binigyang diin ng noo’y SSC Chairperson Frances Daphne Mobo ang seremonya sa paglalahad ng Budget at Accomplishment Reports ng kanyang termino.
Samantala, inamin ni Mobo na nabigo nilang tuparin ang ilang mga pangakong proyekto para sa mga mag-aaral.
“Failures make us better individuals. Leadership teaches you how to be accountable [for] your own actions and how to accept constructive criticism. To our student leaders present here today, do remember that leadership is not about positions or titles because those are temporary. Leadership is about passion and dedication,” aniya.
Bukod dito, binigyan ng pagkakataon ang bagong termino upang talakayin at bigyang linaw ang kanilang mga inihandang proyekto at aktibidades sa harap ng kanilang mga kapwa lider-estudyante.
Sumatutal, mayroong 12 na proyekto at 17 na aktibidades na gustong ilunsad ang naturang termino sa pangunguna ni ngayo’y SSC Chairperson Julier Samonte. Ilan dito ay ang GCLAMP 2.0: Enhancing Access in the GCLAMP, CODE 200: Tech Industry Partnership Found, BIN-novation: Classroom for a Cleaner Future para sa kanilang proyekto, samantala ay inahain naman ang Student First Starter Packs: College Life Unboxed, Think Green: Green Revolutions for GCians, Beyond the Myths: Understanding HIV/AIDS & Prevention para sa kanilang mga aktibidades.
Ngunit ang lahat ng kanilang programa ay pawang mga panukala pa lamang at posibleng mayroong ibang hindi mapayagang mailunsad, paglilinaw ng bagong konseho.
Bilang karagdagan sa mga pangako ni Samonte, bagong kinatawan ng konseho, nais niya at ng kaniyang konseho na palawakin ang komunikasyon at transparency sa pagsasagawa ng regular student consultations, open forums, at online suggestion platforms para marinig ang tunay na saloobin ng mga estudyante. Kasama rin dito ang paglabas ng general plan of actions, budget breakdowns, at accomplishment reports upang mas mapanatili ang tiwala ng bawat estudyante sa konseho.
“Pangatlo, mas makabuluhang events para sa lahat. Gagawin nating inclusive at meaningful ang bawat SSC-led event. Hindi lang ito basta kasiyahan dahil gagawa rin po tayo ng mga aktibidad na magtataguyod ng leadership, advocacy, at student development.”
“Pang apat, pagpapalakas ng bawat student organization sa bawat departamento. Bilang inyong chairperson, sisiguraduhin kong makakaramdam ng buong suporta ang bawat departamental organization,” dagdag pa niya.
Paalala naman ng bagong SSC Chairperson na ang kaniyang pagkapanalo noong nakaraang eleksyon ay hindi lamang tagumpay ng isang tao kundi tagumpay rin ng bawat estudyante na naniniwala na pwedeng marinig, makilahok, at maging bahagi ng pagbabago.
\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
Ang The Forefront, opisyal na publikasyon ng Dalubhasaan, ay mananatiling nakamasid sa mga isasakatuparang programa’t proyekto ng Supreme Student Council na magpapatunay na [#NoStudentLeftBehind](https://www.facebook.com/hashtag/nostudentleftbehind?__eep__=6&__cft__[0]=AZUkpyVY36DTjuBX_LBfWl1h5OP6SP8zSgH13ZOCR57qpBG2lvcnA_I0eP3OwXk_sxf7aRgZa69lsUJjmMQHbHu8xs2v1a0HnCWW9WXGSwEHAGPaerl4e8IKYXgllMeCrIKjYNpwvOkmG3U8rOsk_wNIfahDcxsTgloutQi60oQnSFHiFr3KfpSyDtumfUmdLUc&__tn__=*NK-R).

[Image: 𝐃𝐫. 𝐏𝐚𝐠𝐮𝐢𝐨 𝐨𝐧 𝐆𝐂’𝐬 𝐌𝐚𝐜 𝐋𝐚𝐛: 𝟏𝐬𝐭 𝐢𝐧 𝐙𝐚𝐦𝐛𝐚𝐥𝐞𝐬, 𝟏𝐬𝐭 𝐢𝐧 𝐑𝐞𝐠𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐈𝐈𝐈]
[Campus](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/campus/) [CCS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/ccs/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Thursday, May 22, 2025**
Gordon College (GC), through the College of Computer Studies (CCS), opened its Mac Laboratory on the 5th floor of the building, room 507, last April 28.
This new facility aims to equip students with the necessary competencies to keep up with the quick growth of technological advancements.
According to Dr. Imelda D.P. Soriano, the College President, the Mac Laboratory aims to innovate the school through integrating smart classrooms over the years and to, ideally, attract international students, preparing the students for the evolution of Gordon College into a university.
Meanwhile, in a previous report by The Forefront, students from CCS initiated the GC CCS Tech Fest as a fundraising event last February for the improvements of the Mac Lab room and some of its equipment.
When interviewed again, Kurt Jeehan Terado, one of the event’s heads, expressed his joy because their collective effort as students manifested in their school.
“Knowing that we were able to contribute something that future students will benefit from, I feel like everyone from our batch felt fulfilled,” said Terado.
To add to their efforts, the Mac Lab units came from the Local Government Unit (LGU) as the funds raised through this event are for the sole-purpose of room improvements.
Moreover, Dr. Darwin Paguio, Vice President of Academic Affairs, declared his pride for GC’s achievement.
“The first local college na magkaka-Mac laboratory. [The] first in zambales, [the] first in region 3,” Dr. Paguio stated.
With GC’s climb towards innovation, the institution also eyes international deployment of its students in Macau.

# CEAS Mentors Duo Reclaims Gold, Overpowers CBA in Mixed Doubles Badminton Clash
[Image: CEAS Mentors Duo Reclaims Gold, Overpowers CBA in Mixed Doubles Badminton Clash]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, September 17, 2025**
TRICKSHOT COVERED COURT—In a smashing display of speed, precision, and teamwork, the College of Education, Arts and Sciences (CEAS) Mentors reclaimed badminton glory, dominating the mixed doubles championship against the College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) Wizards on September 16, 2025, at the Trickshot Badminton Court.
Right from the opening serve, the dynamic duo of Daniel Ruzzel Bernal and Mary Anne Lafuente wasted no time asserting their dominance with lightning-quick reflexes and razor-sharp shot placements.
Consequently, their rapid-fire rallies and clever net play kept the CBA Wizards scrambling across the court, ending the first set in convincing fashion at 21-9, with CEAS clearly in control.
Building on that first-set momentum, Bernal and Lafuente came out swinging in the second set, smashing their way to an early lead through seamless communication and smart positioning.
Meanwhile, although the Wizards attempted a comeback with aggressive clears and pushes, every move was countered by the Mentors’ calculated drop shots, steady defense, and powerful smashes, closing the set at 21-1 to secure the championship crown.
After the final rally, Lafuente emphasized that the victory was not only a triumph of skill but also a testament to their trust in each other, both on and off the court.
Similarly, Bernal expressed his pride and excitement after clinching the gold, saying, “Sobrang saya po and super proud po ako sa partner ko.”
Before reaching the finals, the duo battled past the CHTM Sharks in the elimination round, overcoming sharp smashes and relentless returns to secure a 30–10 win and a spot in the championship stage.
Furthermore, the CEAS pair showed that age and experience were no barriers, as their first match as college students turned into a golden opportunity to prove their fire and determination. | via Lovely Mae M. Doroin/The FOREFRONTFOREFRONT

# GC Welcomes Konyang University of South Korea
[Image: GC Welcomes Konyang University of South Korea]
[IPDEA](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/ipdea/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, May 14, 2025**
On May 14, 2025, Gordon College warmly welcomed delegates from Konyang University, South Korea, as part of an initiative to explore academic collaboration between the two institutions. The delegation was composed of Ms. Youngmi Sohn, Head of the Global Education Innovation Headquarters, and Mr. Dong-Yeon Ko, Team Leader.
The visit aimed to strengthen international ties, particularly in the field of Allied Health Studies, and included a tour of the college’s facilities—classrooms, laboratories, and library—especially in relation to Konyang’s interest in developing a short-term language program.
During the meeting, the delegates expressed their interest in discussing various opportunities for collaboration and confirmed that they are currently reviewing the proposed Memorandum of Agreement (MOA). Both institutions expressed optimism for a sustainable and meaningful partnership that supports global academic engagement and student development.

# Clean As You Go & Bring Your Own Trash Policy
[Image: Clean As You Go & Bring Your Own Trash Policy]
**Friday, August 8, 2025**
Instructions for Gordon College Students: Clean As You Go & Bring Your Own Trash Policy
Purpose:
To foster a clean and respectful campus environment while promoting sustainability through personal responsibility for waste.
Before Class or Activity:
1. Prepare Your Materials:
– Bring Your Own Trash: Carry a small, reusable bag or container to collect any waste you generate during class or activities. This could be a sturdy tote, a lunch bag, or any suitable container.
– Plan for Snacks/Drinks: If you plan to consume food or beverages, pack them in a way that minimizes waste (e.g., reusable containers, bottles).
During Class or Activity:
2. Maintain Your Space:
– Keep Your Area Clean: Throughout the session, stay vigilant about your surroundings. Avoid leaving papers, wrappers, or any personal items strewn about.
– Immediate Clean-Up: If you create a mess (accidental spills, paper cuts, etc.), address it promptly. If cleaning supplies are available, use them to tidy up immediately.
3. Responsible Waste Management:
– Use Your Reusable Container: Whenever you have waste, place it in your reusable bag or container. Keep it nearby for easy access.
– Respect Shared Spaces: Be mindful of not just your space but also those around you. Dispose of any common area waste (e.g., napkins from community snacks) in your own trash.
After Class or Activity:
4. Final Cleanup:
– Inspect Your Area:
Before leaving, take a moment to ensure your seating area and any communal spaces (tables, chairs) are tidy.
– Empty Your Trash into Appropriate Bins: When you leave the classroom or activity, empty your reusable container at designated trash or recycling bins outside the classroom (if available). Take anything that belongs to you with you.
5. Dispose Responsibly:
– Recycle When Possible: If your waste is recyclable, place it in the recycling bin. Be sure to check the college’s recycling guidelines to ensure proper disposal.
Additional Responsibilities:
6. Encourage a Culture of Cleanliness:
– Remind Peers:If you notice a classmate leaving a mess, gently remind them or offer to help.
– Lead by Example: Demonstrating your commitment to the “Clean As You Go” policy can inspire your peers to do the same.
7. Feedback and Improvement:
– Participate in discussions or feedback sessions organized by the college to share your insights on how the Clean As You Go policy is working.
8. Engage with Initiatives:
– Stay updated on any college-led environmental initiatives or clean-up days, and participate actively when you can.
By adhering to these instructions, you contribute to creating a cleaner, more sustainable campus environment while developing valuable habits of personal responsibility and community stewardship. Thank you for doing your part!

# Fostering Mental Health
[Image: Fostering Mental Health]
[Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/extension/) [RDCES](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/rdces/) [Community Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/community-extension/)
**Wednesday, July 26, 2023**
In adherence to our commitment to community service, the GC Community Extension Services Unit (GC-CESU), in collaboration with the GC Guidance Office and Tapinac Senior High School, successfully organized and conducted a seminar on July 31, 2023. The seminar aimed to enhance the resilience of the attendees by equipping them with techniques to address and manage challenges, emphasizing the importance of self-care, addressing mental health stigma, and facilitating access to mental health services. Ms. Mechel Bautista, GC Guidance Office Head, served as the resource speaker. The seminar saw active participation from 42 teachers from Tapinac Senior High School and took place at the Gordon College function hall.

# GC Color Run Kicks Off 27th Founding Anniversary
[Image: GC Color Run Kicks Off 27th Founding Anniversary]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Sunday, February 22, 2026**
The annual GC FUNdation RUN marked the start of Gordon College’s 27th Founding Anniversary, held on February 22, 2026, at the East Tapinac Oval Track.
The event gathered members of the Gordon College Faculty Association (GCFAS), administrative staff, students, alumni, and community participants under the pre-foundation day celebration motif, “Color Run.”
The program commenced with the assembly of participants, followed by an opening ceremony that featured welcome messages from Dr. Katherine Yap and Dr. Imelda D.P. Soriano. A Zumba warm-up session, led by administrators, faculty members, and students from the Bachelor of Physical Education (BPED), energized attendees ahead of the race.
The run officially started at 6:30 AM as participants filled the track dressed in white shirts and athletic attire, ready to be splashed with vibrant colored powder and water.
More than a display of color and movement, this year’s FUNdation RUN is a colorful display of the institution’s commitment to promoting wellness, unity, and community relations while marking the upcoming foundation celebration with lively and memorable moments. | via Sam Andrea R. # WARRIORS’ CARNAGE: GC Bags Second Championship Streak at LCUAA National Games
[Image: WARRIORS’ CARNAGE: GC Bags Second Championship Streak at LCUAA National Games]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Saturday, October 25, 2025**
After six days of grueling competition, the Gordon College (GC) Warriors are hailed as the ultimate victors of the 14th Local Colleges and Universities Athletic Association (LCUAA) National Games, marking GC’s second winning streak as the overall champion.
Across several categories, the Warriors collectively bagged 31 gold, 22 silver, and 24 bronze medals across their LCUAA stint, a resounding sweep above overall 2nd place Kolehiyo ng Lungsod ng Lipa’s (KLL) 23 gold, 18 silver, and 21 bronze medal tally.
Meanwhile, the overall 3rd place title was claimed by Pamantasan ng Lungsod ng Pasig (PLP) with 15 gold, 13 silver, and 15 bronze medals across their run.
Furthermore, GC brought home championship plaques in the swimming, dancesport, and taekwondo categories awarded today, October 25, at the CSJDM Sports Complex. The Olongapo-reps were also awarded plaques for 1st runner-up in badminton, chess, and men’s futsal, as well as; 2nd runner-up in athletics.
The Warriors are set to defend their winning streak at next year’s national games at Binoloan, Pangasinan, hosted by the University of Eastern Pangasinan (UEP). | via Lance Isaac Leon/The FOREFRONT

# Gordon College Celebrates World Teachers’ Day
[Image: Gordon College Celebrates World Teachers’ Day]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Tuesday, October 7, 2025**
GORDON COLLEGE MAIN BLDG—The Office of the Student Welfare and Services (OSWS) hosted a World Teachers’ Day Celebration, together with various student organizations, which aims to honor the dedicated instructors of the institution on October 6, 2025, at Gordon College Main Bldg—Function Hall.
The event began with an opening prayer and remarks from Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano, Gordon College President, and Dr. Darwin P. Paguio, Vice President for Academic Affairs.
“We cannot have a lavish lifestyle in teaching. But your dedication and passion will definitely [inaudible] through saying our students’ success— changing their lives, especially those who are underprivileged students, and you are making them a story,” said Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano, showing her gratitude for the effort of Gordon College’s faculty.
Dr. Darwin P. Paguio also expressed to the faculty that the celebration isn’t just about their perseverance, but also to take care of all the hard work in the classrooms as they invest in nurturing curiosity that will nourish them for a lifetime.
After the opening ceremony, Performing Arts Students Ensemble (PASE) Voci tributed a chorale performance that honors the educators.
To spark joy with the event, the College of Allied Health Studies (CAHS) Student Council, Midwifery Student Organization (MSO), and the Nursing Student Organization (NSO) hosted games to energize and entertain the teachers.
Furthermore, the PASE Ballo sparked warmth and embrace with a performance number.
Moreover, the Supreme Student Council (SSC) and Human Resource Management Unit (HRMU) conducted an awarding ceremony to appreciate the faculty’s skills with admiration and humor.
Meanwhile, Hon. Rolen Paulino Jr., City Mayor of Olongapo, held a speech and cash raffle for the faculty.
“Salamat sa sacrifices niyo, sa mahabang pasensiya ninyo, tiyaka sa lahat ng ginagawa niyo para sa mga students natin,” Paulino stated.
An Audio-Visual Presentation (AVP) video was shown to the faculty, featuring greetings and messages from the students, followed by the students who presented gifts to their beloved tutors.
Lastly, another raffle event was held with prizes for the faculty, which filled the room with joy and laughter.
The said event was supposed to be celebrated on October 3rd but was rescheduled due to Typhoon Paolo.| via Jasmine Zeah Llagas/The FOREFRONT

𝐓𝐮𝐥𝐨𝐧𝐠 𝐃𝐮𝐧𝐨𝐧𝐠 𝐏𝐫𝐨𝐠𝐫𝐚𝐦 (𝐓𝐃𝐏) 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐓𝐞𝐫𝐭𝐢𝐚𝐫𝐲 𝐄𝐝𝐮𝐜𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐒𝐮𝐛𝐬𝐢𝐝𝐲 (𝐓𝐄𝐒) 𝐃𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐫𝐢𝐛𝐮𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧
To all 𝐥𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐝 𝐓𝐃𝐏 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐓𝐄𝐒 𝐠𝐫𝐚𝐧𝐭𝐞𝐞𝐬, please be advised that the claiming of your financial grants will be held at 𝐆𝐨𝐫𝐝𝐨𝐧 𝐂𝐨𝐥𝐥𝐞𝐠𝐞, 𝐅𝐮𝐧𝐜𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐇𝐚𝐥𝐥 (𝟑𝐫𝐝 𝐅𝐥𝐨𝐨𝐫) 𝐨𝐧 𝐃𝐞𝐜𝐞𝐦𝐛𝐞𝐫 𝟏𝟏–𝟏𝟐, 𝟐𝟎𝟐𝟓, 𝐟𝐫𝐨𝐦 𝟕:𝟎𝟎 𝐀𝐌 𝐭𝐨 𝟓:𝟎𝟎 𝐏𝐌.
This payout covers the following:
• 𝐓𝐃𝐏 𝐁𝐚𝐭𝐜𝐡 𝟕.𝟏 – 𝟐𝐧𝐝 𝐒𝐞𝐦𝐞𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐫, 𝐀𝐜𝐚𝐝𝐞𝐦𝐢𝐜 𝐘𝐞𝐚𝐫 𝟐𝟎𝟐𝟑–𝟐𝟎𝟐𝟒
• 𝐓𝐃𝐏 𝐁𝐚𝐭𝐜𝐡 𝟑 – 𝟏𝐬𝐭 𝐒𝐞𝐦𝐞𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐫, 𝐀𝐜𝐚𝐝𝐞𝐦𝐢𝐜 𝐘𝐞𝐚𝐫 𝟐𝟎𝟐𝟒–𝟐𝟎𝟐𝟓
• 𝐓𝐄𝐒 𝐋𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐡𝐚𝐧𝐚𝐧 𝟏𝟓 – 𝟏𝐬𝐭 𝐒𝐞𝐦𝐞𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐫, 𝐀𝐜𝐚𝐝𝐞𝐦𝐢𝐜 𝐘𝐞𝐚𝐫 𝟐𝟎𝟐𝟒–𝟐𝟎𝟐𝟓
[Image: 📄]
𝐑𝐞𝐪𝐮𝐢𝐫𝐞𝐝 𝐃𝐨𝐜𝐮𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐬
Please prepare the following complete documents :
1. 𝐓𝐡𝐫𝐞𝐞 (𝟑) 𝐩𝐡𝐨𝐭𝐨𝐜𝐨𝐩𝐢𝐞𝐬 of your 𝐬𝐭𝐮𝐝𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐈𝐃 𝐨𝐫 𝐯𝐚𝐥𝐢𝐝 𝐠𝐨𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐧𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐈𝐃 (𝐰𝐢𝐭𝐡 𝐬𝐢𝐠𝐧𝐚𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞), each with 𝐭𝐡𝐫𝐞𝐞 (𝟑) 𝐬𝐩𝐞𝐜𝐢𝐦𝐞𝐧 𝐬𝐢𝐠𝐧𝐚𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞𝐬
2. 𝐅𝐨𝐫 𝐀𝐮𝐭𝐡𝐨𝐫𝐢𝐳𝐞𝐝 𝐑𝐞𝐩𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐯𝐞𝐬 (𝐈𝐦𝐦𝐞𝐝𝐢𝐚𝐭𝐞 𝐅𝐚𝐦𝐢𝐥𝐲 𝐎𝐍𝐋𝐘) — all in 𝐭𝐡𝐫𝐞𝐞 (𝟑) 𝐜𝐨𝐩𝐢𝐞𝐬:
a. Original 𝐀𝐮𝐭𝐡𝐨𝐫𝐢𝐳𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐋𝐞𝐭𝐭𝐞𝐫 with the grantee’s wet signature.
b. Photocopy of the 𝐬𝐭𝐮𝐝𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐈𝐃 𝐨𝐫 𝐯𝐚𝐥𝐢𝐝 𝐠𝐨𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐧𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐈𝐃 (with signature) of the grantee, each with 𝐭𝐡𝐫𝐞𝐞 (𝟑) 𝐬𝐩𝐞𝐜𝐢𝐦𝐞𝐧 𝐬𝐢𝐠𝐧𝐚𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞𝐬.
c. Photocopy of the 𝐯𝐚𝐥𝐢𝐝 𝐠𝐨𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐧𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐈𝐃 (with signature) of the authorized representative, each with 𝐭𝐡𝐫𝐞𝐞 (𝟑) 𝐬𝐩𝐞𝐜𝐢𝐦𝐞𝐧 𝐬𝐢𝐠𝐧𝐚𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞𝐬.
3. 𝐓𝐄𝐒 𝐆𝐫𝐚𝐧𝐭𝐞𝐞𝐬 𝐎𝐧𝐥𝐲

1. Please bring 𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐥𝐞𝐭𝐞 𝐝𝐨𝐜𝐮𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐬 and strictly follow your 𝐚𝐬𝐬𝐢𝐠𝐧𝐞𝐝 𝐬𝐜𝐡𝐞𝐝𝐮𝐥𝐞 to avoid crowding.

# GC Welcomes 5 new Registered Midwifes, Perfect passing in November MLE
[Image: GC Welcomes 5 new Registered Midwifes, Perfect passing in November MLE]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, November 19, 2025**
Five alumni from the College of Allied Health and Services (CAHS) of Gordon College (GC) passed the Midwives Licensure Exam (MLE) according to the Professional Regulation Commission Board.
GC achieved a perfect passing rate of 100% with 5 passers out of 5 examinees, once again surpassing the rate of last year’s results of 93.48% and the national rate of 57.60%. They constitute the 2,152 newly registered midwives.
All are first-time takers of this year’s exam.
Additionally, Tagaloan Community College is the top-performing school in the licensure exam with a perfect passing rate from 85 examinees of the school.
Meanwhile, Princesa Dumale Talamayan, this year’s board topnotcher, from Palawan State University achieved a 94.00%.

[Image: 𝐈𝐝𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐭𝐲 𝐍𝐨𝐰 𝐖𝐨𝐫𝐧 𝐢𝐧 𝐂𝐚𝐦𝐩𝐮𝐬: 𝐆𝐂 𝐈𝐧𝐭𝐫𝐨𝐝𝐮𝐜𝐞𝐬 𝐒𝐎𝐆𝐈𝐄-𝐈𝐧𝐜𝐥𝐮𝐬𝐢𝐯𝐞 𝐔𝐧𝐢𝐟𝐨𝐫𝐦 𝐏𝐨𝐥𝐢𝐜𝐲]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Tuesday, August 5, 2025**
The Office of Student Welfare and Services (OSWS) released an advisory on July 31, 2025, regarding the implementation of a new uniform policy that formally recognizes the right of students to wear components of the existing prescribed uniform that align with their Sexual Orientation, Gender Identity, Gender Expression, and Sex Characteristics (SOGIESC).
This initiative aims to provide a space for members of the LGBTQIA+ community to express their authentic selves freely within the campus.
According to the advisory, the implementation of the proper wearing of uniforms, including this new provision, will start on September 8, 2025, for freshmen and transferees.
Meanwhile, for continuing students, the change will take effect on August 18, 2025. The wearing of the prescribed uniform will remain compulsory for all students from Monday to Thursday.
“In recognition of the spectrum of gender identities within our student body, the institution affirms the right of individuals to express their authentic selves through their attire,” the institution affirmed in its official statement.
Furthermore, on “wash days” (Friday, Saturday, Sunday, and Mid-Year Term), students have the right to choose their attire, as long as it adheres to the College’s prescribed school attire and maintains decency.
This means the students are now allowed to choose from the established guidelines for male and female clothing, specifically, the pants or skirts that are already part of the school’s approved uniform, to express their gender identity best, provided the chosen attire still adheres to the College’s overall dress code and decency standards.
The said office encourages the entire Gordon College community to ensure the implementation of this new policy.
